hypothesis: If tensor completion methods (CPD-S, NeAT, and their ensemble aggregated via a random forest) are used as surrogate models to predict Young's modulus and mass-normalized Young's modulus from the structured lattice-design tensor, then they will achieve higher predictive accuracy (higher mean R² and lower variance) than baseline ML models (Gaussian Process, XGBoost, and MLP), particularly when training data are limited or non-uniformly sampled, because tensor completion exploits the multi-way structure and correlations across design modes to better generalize from sparse or biased observations.
